text: A CT scan reveals a large lesion in the right lobe of the liver, predominantly located to the right of the middle hepatic vein. The surgeon begins the right hepatectomy with a reverse L incision on the right side of the abdomen, followed by the division of the falciform ligament after laparotomy.

The surgeon clamps, cuts, and suture ties the structure containing the umbilical vein. Retractors are applied, and the falciform ligament is divided while pressing the liver down with the left hand, revealing the suprahepatic inferior vena cava. The division occurs in an avascular plane.
The surgeon retracts the liver upwards and removes the gallbladder, carefully detaching omental adhesions from the gallbladder surface.
The surgeon employs a fundus-first approach to remove the gallbladder from its bed, identifying the cystic duct and artery, which are then ligated and divided.

The surgeon retracts the liver upward with the left hand and divides the attachments of the liver to the Girotus fascia. A small vein connecting the caudate lobe to the intrahepatic inferior vena cava is clipped and divided to prevent hemorrhage during mobilization of the liver's inferior surface.

The surgeon focuses on the lateral part of the liver, mobilizing the right triangular ligaments. The liver is retracted upwards, revealing the tumor attached to the diaphragmatic surface. The surgeon uses a long Kelly to hold the diaphragm while the assistant retracts the liver towards the left side and superiorly, facilitating the dissection of the right triangular ligaments.

The surgeon divides the right triangular ligaments of the liver, working within an avascular plane. The liver is carefully retracted to avoid tearing from the inferior vena cava, emphasizing the importance of traction and counter-traction during this step.

After dividing the right triangular ligaments, the retrohepatic inferior vena cava (IVC) becomes visible. The surgeon methodically mobilizes the liver from the retrohepatic IVC by tying, clipping, and dividing the short hepatic veins that connect the liver to the IVC, focusing on the caudate lobe.

The surgeon exposes three-fourths of the circumference of the inferior vena cava (IVC) using liger clips to secure veins before division. The hepatocava ligament, a bridge between the right and left hemilivers behind the IVC, is divided using hydro-dissection to expose the right hepatic vein. A right angle is passed from below, and dissection continues from above, creating a plane for looping the right hepatic vein with umbilical tape. The tape is positioned above and below the IVC and liver, facilitating the hanging of the liver. The caudate process is cut to allow the tape to sit in the groove, forming the plane of dissection.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the caudate process, completing the mobilization of the liver.

The surgeon performs hilar dissection using the Glissonian pedicle approach, retracting the liver to expose segment four. The peritoneum is divided below segment four, creating a plane for lowering the hilar plate through blunt and sharp dissection. The hilar plate is pressed down while segment four is retracted upwards, revealing a clear dissection plane.

The surgeon divides the cystic plate located between the gallbladder bed and the hilum to access the right glissonian pedicle. Using a right angle, the entire right glissonian pedicle, including the right portal vein, right hepatic artery, and right bile duct, is looped. A temporary clamp is applied, causing a visible color change between the right and left liver, marking the line of demarcation.

The surgeon lifts the liver and marks the transection plane on its inferior surface, ensuring it is positioned just to the right of the middle hepatic vein. The marking extends to the right glissonian pedicle, delineating the area for resection.

The transaction plane is visibly marked, showing a clear demarcation between the right and left liver. The surgeon prepares for the Pringle maneuver by passing a finger through the foramen of Winslow and placing a snugger around the hilar structures, aiming to temporarily clamp the portal structures to reduce blood loss during the liver transaction.

The surgeon begins the parenchymal transection of the liver using a harmonic scalpel along the previously marked line of demarcation. The initial two to three centimeters of the liver are transected in a safe plane, free from major blood vessels or bile ducts.

The surgeon retracts the liver upward and begins dividing the inferior part of the liver using a harmonic device for the initial two centimeters of the parenchymal transaction. After completing this section, the surgeon switches to a CUSA device, an ultrasonic tool with irrigation and suction capabilities, which selectively removes liver parenchymal tissue while preserving ducts and blood vessels.

The surgeon uses a CUSA device for liver parenchymal transaction while the assistant operates a monopolar diathermy set to high settings in spray mode. Blood vessels are defined and clipped using Liga clips. Due to bleeding, the Pringle maneuver is applied, temporarily clamping the hilar structures to control blood inflow, with a cycle of ten minutes on and five minutes off during which transaction pauses.

The surgeon releases the Pringle maneuver after ten minutes, allowing the liver to rest for a five-minute hemostatic pause. Following this pause, the Pringle maneuver is reapplied to block the blood supply, and the liver transection continues.

The surgeon performs the liver transection in a straight line, using the CUSA device to follow the middle hepatic vein, which is kept on the left side to preserve it. Small blood vessels are coagulated, while larger ones are clipped and divided, and even larger veins are ligated, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon ligates vessels on the specimen side, clips, and divides them. After completing the transaction, the surgeon uses the hanging maneuver with umbilical tape passed through the right hepatic vein, behind the liver, and in front of the inferior vena cava to lift and suspend the liver.

The surgeon lifts and hangs the liver to direct the dissection plane, carefully avoiding injury to short hepatic veins and the inferior vena cava. The liver parenchyma is mostly divided, and attention is turned to the right Glissonian pedicle, which is looped and prepared for stapling.
The surgeon uses an Endo GIA tan cartridge vascular stapler to staple the entire right-sided Glissonian pedicle. Above the stapled pedicle, another vein is identified, ligated, and divided.

The surgeon completes the liver transaction using the hanging technique, lifting the umbilical tape from both below and above until it is removed, indicating the transaction is complete. The right hepatic vein remains as the final structure holding the liver, which is then stapled using a white vascular stapler.

The surgeon clamps, divides, and sutures the right hepatic vein, allowing for the extraction of the liver specimen. There is a small bleeding from one of the short hepatic veins near the exposed inferior vena cava, which is controlled. The inferior vena cava is visibly exposed, with more than three-fourths of its surface visible.
The right hepatectomy is completed, revealing the staple line from the division of the Glissonian pedicle. A wash is applied to the surgical area.

The surgeon fixes the left lobe of the liver to the falciform ligament using multiple sutures to prevent it from falling into the space created by the removal of the right lobe. Hemostasis is achieved, and the abdomen is closed without placing any drains.
The surgical closure is completed, and the specimen from the right hepatectomy is displayed, showing a large tumor occupying all liver segments with a clear transaction margin.
